summary.rationale: Mixed employee sentiment; moderate innovation; emerging data culture; high AI awareness; 40 reviews

reviews.title: Fast Paced
reviews.pros: Benefits, coworkers, new technology, remote
reviews.cons: Fear of layoffs, competition, can be long hours

reviews.title: Open, fast, no bs
reviews.pros:
It might be easy to roll your eyes when people from Facebook say how open their culture is, but it's true; it's more open than any other place I've worked at. At a company wide-level, secret projects, public incidents, important non-public business metrics and the like are all openly discussed. You can ask questions about them directly to Zuckerburg at the weekly Q&A. I think the idea is that if everyone is on the same page or at least, differing views are heard, the company will be stronger, and solutions may be offered from a place you didn't expect. This is much different from previous companies I worked at, where discussions on internal email lists would be shut down by some lawyer saying that there's certain things that can't be discussed, and important data is divided up to groups and individuals on a "need to know" basis, etc. 

This culture applies at a lower level too. You feel comfortable giving feedback to each other about each other, about product decisions, about management, etc. The flipside of this openness is that you of course, have to be willing to receive the feedback, you have to recognize that while openness and feedback is highly encouraged, decisions have to get made, and actions and data are more valuable than words. At the higher level, since the company trusts employees with access to so much information, keeping such info confidential from the outside world is taken seriously.

It's a great place to work as an engineer. You're given a lot of freedom, but it's also a responsibility to make sure you're doing things that are valuable. You don't get much credit for working hard or being smart if you don't produce valuable output.

One cool thing about Facebook, in contrast to other comparable companies (Google, Amazon, and Microsoft, though in truth, FB is much smaller), is how they have a pretty singular focus. Even with the differing areas (including advertising and such), they do a good job of keeping their eye on their mission of connecting as many people as possible. I also think they are way more empathetic to their users than most people give them credit for. It seems like popular opinion has it that FB is arrogant and only cares about its users insomuch as they represent $-signs. From within the company, it didn't feel this way at all. I saw a lot of empathy towards users, and a lot effort spent to improve or things or fix broken things with no direct financial benefit. The strategy is not complex. The thinking is that if they can make FB easier and more fun to use, then more people will use it for more time each day (which will also have a network effect of attracting even more people to use it), then the advertising dollars will follow. Of course, it is true that FB wants to make the audience more accessible to advertisers as well, but there are a lot of people at FB who care about privacy and security.

They have really good infrastructure and really great ways to share the infrastructure and code. They have a lot of cool internal tools, and what they've built is really impressive, and more importantly, it helps your team build products faster without having to solve problems that someone else already solved. Every software company tries to do this, but FB seems to have been more successful with it. Perhaps it's because they're still relatively small, but if anything, I can at least say it is very cool while it lasts.

The perks and work environment are great, unless you're one of those types that can't stand open office spaces. I've worked in both a private office and open offices in multiple companies. While I do think a private office has some benefits, I mostly think it's a personal luxury for the employee and a huge waste of money for the company. I'd much rather have the money go into other areas like salary, benefits, and other workplace improvements rather than the added real estate necessary to have offices.

Of course, you've heard about the food and snacks. They have an amazing selection of great stuff, and what I like about it is that it sort of goes above and beyond expectations. Sure, some days, lunch is better than others, but I really can't complain, and the selection of drinks and snacks is amazing. It's not like you should work at FB just because of that, but it demonstrates FB's desire to make work as fun and convenient as possible.

You'll be surrounded by people who like being there. I can't think of a better environment to work in. If you have a giant ego, you may not like it as much. Respect is definitely given to those that have deep experience in the industry, and they are expected to lead others and mentor more junior employees. However, if for whatever reason, you can't perform at the level expected, no one is going to care if you did this and that at Google or shipped ten things at MS, etc.

FB also has a lot of fun events, and I made a lot of friends working there, so going to the events was fun. Also, if you're older and worried that FB is just a bunch of 22-year-olds, and that you won't fit in, I wouldn't worry about that. FB does have a lot of young employees (who are really smart btw), and it does hire a lot of people straight out of college, but it also attracts a lot of experienced engineers from other top companies like Google, MS, Amazon, etc.

Work-life-balance seemed totally normal to me. It may be different depending on your team, but I felt you could do 40-50 hours of work a week for the most part and you would be totally fine. It's about what you produce, not how hard you're working. Other team members who had children would work normal hours and go home at normal times. I didn't see any of these folks have a problem when they left early to take care of their child or things like that. Of course, there could be times that people are expected to work extra if something critical happens, but for the most part everyone wants to avoid this and this happens sparingly, from what I observed. Now, there were many times where I chose to work late myself, but I never felt any pressure to do so. The caveat is that there are on-call rotations, and in addition, even if you are not on-call, you are expected to be reasonably available if the on-call person needs your help. However, again, no one wants this, and your team will work on ways to avoid these situations.

The best thing I can say is that working at FB is about productivity. I didn't experience and political bs and it was a pleasure working with a group of people who were all concerned with producing a good product and making the best of the time spent while doing it.
reviews.cons:
FB expects a lot out of engineers, and you can't slack off. Of course, you shouldn't slack off at any job, but since FB is pretty fast-paced, there is a risk that you'll have trouble adjusting at first.

FB has a lot of custom infrastructure and tools, and prehaps more impressively, it works great. It makes doing your job really great, but on the other hand, you'll end up learning a lot of stuff that won't be applicable elsewhere.

FB's code-base is very good in some ways, but in other ways, it's not as great as some of the existing engineers think it is. I don't think this is that big of a deal, but the important part is that as an engineer, you need to quickly learn FB's values and practices and "get with the program" so to speak. If you don't like some things, then you just have to deal with it, as it's not likely you're going to change people's minds at this point. The nice thing is that things are at least very consistent.
reviews.advice: You need to focus on how you are going to maintain the existing culture and protocols as you grow. I think this could be really tough.

reviews.title: Fast paced company with high expectations, but incredibly fair. You won't a place that cares more about it's people.
reviews.pros:
- Incredible benefits 
- Excellent compensation that rewards strong performance 
- Lots of autonomy 
- Tons of room for growth
- Very transparent from the top down
- Strong leadership
- Intelligent and caring colleagues
- The most fair and well thought out review process you will find everywhere 
- Exciting work
reviews.cons:
- If you don't learn to make the work life balance work for you, it can be exhausting. But you'll also be given lots of support with this if you ask for it. 
- It doesn't matter how good you are at your job, chances are you're going to be surrounded by a ton of other people who are just as good if not better. Imposter syndrome is real, but if you got an offer, you probably belong here too.
reviews.advice: Keep doing what you're doing, keep an eye on employee burnout, keep supporting your people and keep pushing them, and the rest of the world, forward.

reviews.title: Pleasantly Surprised
reviews.pros:
I'm a 55 year old working in a company where the average age is 28, and I've never been happier. I have always wanted to work in a company where I wasn't the proverbial square peg, and after nearly 30 years in the work force, I believe that I have found the company that complements my ideology for how a company should operate. Here are the "Pros" as I see them...

- Leadership:  Mark Zuckerberg, Sheryl Sandberg, Chris Cox and the rest of the leadership team. Inspiring, smart and totally real.
- The Culture:  The openness is a real thing, we're trusted to do the right thing, mistakes are expected, being yourself is important and encouraged, humility is encouraged and expected, building trust is paramount, the mission of making the world a more open and connected place isn't just a cool phrase because it guides everything. It's not about us, it's about everyone else.
- The People:  Smart and nice is what I find. The diversity is better than any other company I've worked at and it's getting better. Those that are figuring out how to make what we do better for everyone are really good at implementing their ideas. Two years ago I didn't know what this company really was or if it had a secure future. I can clearly see now that they have what it takes to last, and it's sincere.
- Management:  Managers are hired because they're good managers and not because they're good at something else. The management track is different than the contributor track and they're equal in importance.
- Benefits:  Working in the Valley it's hard to differentiate all the benefits employees receive and Facebook is at least equal to all the major tech companies, but coming from Seattle the benefits are nothing short of incredible.

Granted, I haven't been with the company for very long and I'm still in the honeymoon period, but I can tell what's real and what's not; this place is for real.
reviews.cons:
Like any tech company, the work is big and complicated so work/life balance can suffer from time to time. The difference is that it's the passion within the individuals that drives them to work long hours and forego time off. Of course there can be times when schedule demands force the imbalance, but it's not such that 10-12 hours days become commonplace and expected regardless. We do what we do to get the job done; no one is killing themselves because there's an order to do so.

Being able to eat to your hearts/stomachs content can be dangerous to the waistline, but that's a self-control thing and not the company's issue.
reviews.advice: Keep doing what you're doing and fight hard to not be complacent. I believe that humility is the key so don't ever lose that thinking. Your motives will always be suspect, but if you're able to look in the mirror everyday and know that your motives are pure, that's all that matters. Walking the talk is the best evidence there is.

reviews.title: People Focused
reviews.pros: Facebook deeply cares about its employees and has built a compelling culture around support and growth. Career growth opportunities are plentiful. If you don't like the team you're on or don't get the support you want from your manger, Facebook empowers you to find new teams or projects. Facebook wants its employees to be invested in their work and to feel connected to its larger mission. If large scale opportunities and growth are important to you, Facebook is a fantastic place to work.
reviews.cons: Facebook's culture is demanding and fast paced. The greatest aspect of working at Facebook is that everyone is very motivated and very smart. The problem with this is that they all expect the very same of you. Holding a very high bar for excellence can certainly be demanding so it's important to make sure you're always carefully paying attention to your own personal work/life balance.

reviews.title: Great with the right team
reviews.pros: Great work life balance, lots of mentorship and learning, cutting edge work, fast career growth, not too much hierarchy, culture of  helping each other and collaboration
reviews.cons: Lots of empire building, increasingly worse politics and reliability issues, people are laser focused on their promotions and not getting fired which erodes culture of learning and collaboration, every year they cut our pay and benefits, very team dependent. I’m incredibly lucky with my team but majority of teams here are toxic and bad and it’s hard to know how your experience will be. Try to stay in infra, and as deep in the stack as you can. Don’t work directly for MSL it’s an absolute shitshow. Don’t work for AI/ML infra or fb or instagram (any product roles/product infra). They’re the first to get hit by layoffs and they also have horrible leadership that whips people like slaves, lots of busywork and little credit, everyone is cut throat and fending for themselves

reviews.title: want to be treated like a disposable worker drone? welcome to the party!
reviews.pros:
nice pay
big tech comforts like microkitchens and free breakfast/lunch
$2000 health and wellness grant is p sick actually
reviews.cons:
where to begin...
- had no choice which team i was placed in. eventually got forced into a position i didn't want and worked on products i dont care about
- management and lead unironically tell you to "ask claude" 99% of the time when you're stuck instead of giving you concrete advice
- company maintains a dashboard of employee-level metrics every half tracking your commit count, lines of code pushed, etc. even if you meet all your project requirements, if you don't meet arbitrary lines of code target you can kiss your job goodbye
- if you hate AI slop you will see it everywhere here
- ever present threat of layoff
reviews.advice: uhh maybe jump off the sinking ship?

reviews.title: Contingent workers suffer the brunt
reviews.pros: Some nice people, some smart people, they seem to have fun there.  Interesting work, hot industry
reviews.cons: Contingent workers do a lot of the work with no recognition while their managers continue to be promoted.  Some teams are better than others, there are truly some jerks and very large egos.  Some people seem to get promoted for no reason.  The culture can be tough if you are over 38!  Very difficult for a CW to become a full timer and the stink never wears off
reviews.advice: Make sure your managers actually know what they are doing and have some minimal managerial skills.

reviews.title: Worklife and miscellaneous
reviews.pros:
The Rooftop Park: MPK 20 and 21 feature a massive 9-acre elevated roof garden with walking trails, quiet nooks, and native plants—perfect for "walk-and-talk" meetings.
"Main Street" Vibe: The Classic Campus resembles a town center, complete with various shops, a sweets shop, and an arcade.
Service Perks: On-site services include a barber/hair salon, dry cleaning and laundry (though now for a fee), and bike repair shops to keep you moving.
reviews.cons:
Stack Ranking & "Met Some": The performance review system (PSC) is rigorous. In recent years, even "Met Some" (a mid-tier rating) has been a trigger for termination in some orgs as the company prioritizes moving out "low performers."
"Post or Die": There is an internal culture of "posturing" on Workplace (Meta's internal social network). Employees often feel they must constantly post updates to prove their impact to leadership, which can lead to performative work rather than deep focus.
AI-Driven Metrics: Starting in 2026, Meta has explicitly tied performance reviews to "AI-driven impact," requiring employees to demonstrate how they are using or building with AI to remain competitive.

reviews.title: Work hard, save hard, ride the wave
reviews.pros: Access to state-of-the-art tooling/resources and methodologies to test new technologies before they're broadly accessible to the field. Extremely smart coworkers, great facilities, fantastic benefits and pay. Meta branding can add to the resume if you're on a team with real impact.
reviews.cons: Review culture is horrible. Performance pressure is constant, and expectations can be crushing depending on the team you join. Success here is highly political, you need a good manager and good relationships with higher-ups in your org. Post-2022, everyone is scared of being laid off and the anxiety + depression is toxic and contagious. There's high churn, and there's a lot of performative signaling to gain favor and stay in the game longer. Depending on your team, you may not work on anything interesting or high-impact. Take a job here if you're willing to work hard for a few months/years, but don't let your lifestyle creep up too much from the pay bump -- save aggressively in case of long-term unemployment if a re-org impacts you.

reviews.title: Fine if you don't mind ignore moral issues
reviews.pros:
–Semi-decent pay 
–Decent benefits
–(Depending on the department) Decent work/life balance
reviews.cons:
–Horrible pay, depending on the department. They were paying some contractors only $24 an hour, even though they required a Bachelors, years of experience, and would not adjust the rate for those living  a HCOL areas.
–Having to overlook tons of glaring unethical decisions. Like when they decided calling an LGBT person mentally ill was no longer a community guideline violation (obviously trying to appease the administration so they can continue trying to be a monopoly), or training AI that will steal people's job/steal from artists, or quieting employees who voiced concerns over AI causing suicide, or any ethical concerns.
–Weirdly secret, had to sign the longest NDA I've ever seen, even when it came to benign things that would not invade company privacy.
–Weird personality worship of Mark
reviews.advice: Stop being so unethical, I remember a time when Facebook used to value diversity and innovation. Now all they care about is money (which is wild, because they already have plenty of it)

reviews.title: Meta: High Impact, High Pressure
reviews.pros: Working at Meta offers top-tier compensation, combining high base salary, bonuses, and substantial RSUs, along with the opportunity to build products at massive scale used by billions of people. It provides strong career growth, high ownership, and exposure to top engineering talent and cutting-edge technologies, especially in AI and large-scale systems, while also adding significant brand value to your CV.
reviews.cons: The environment is high-pressure, with aggressive goals and constant performance evaluation, which can impact work-life balance. Frequent reorganizations, ambiguity, and internal competition are common, requiring strong stakeholder management and resilience. A large part of compensation depends on stock performance, and at higher levels, there is more internal politics. Additionally, the company faces ongoing public scrutiny, which can be a reputational factor.

reviews.title: My experience at Meta
reviews.pros: The data platform is exceptionally robust, significantly enhancing developer velocity by abstracting away infrastructure complexities. The platform team has delivered a comprehensive ecosystem where the core pillars of data engineering—compute, storage, quality, and discovery—are seamlessly integrated. Their intentional design has drastically reduced operational overhead, allowing the engineering team to focus entirely on delivering high-impact data products.
reviews.cons: The primary challenge was the disconnect between strategic mandates—like the top-down AI push—and the operational capacity of the data teams. In nascent product areas, the '0 to 1' build phase was frequently disrupted by changing requirements, leading to high team burnout. I believe that for AI innovation to succeed, there needs to be a distinction in how we resource matured data products versus new initiatives. Without a stabilized foundation, teams in the '0 to 1' phase are forced into a dual-track execution that risks both delivery quality and infrastructure health.
reviews.advice:
I believe there is a significant signal loss between front-line teams and Senior Leadership (Sr. Director+). With a four-layer hierarchy (M1 to D2), it becomes difficult for senior stakeholders to gain true visibility into team-level execution and the quality of decision-making during PSC (Performance Summary Cycle).

Streamlining this structure to a more flattened organization would not only increase operational velocity but also ensure greater accountability. Reducing these layers would prevent 'system gaming' at the middle-management level and foster a more transparent, meritocratic culture where senior leaders have a direct pulse on product impact.

reviews.title: Pay is good, unstable environment
reviews.pros: Pay is good. Benefit is good.
reviews.cons: Only look for short term impact due to psc. Infra is bad, everything is constantly broken, as no body cares about reliability. Scope overlap, folks who can manage up survives, org is super bloated but people who do real work be burned out and pushed out.
reviews.advice: Have long term vision, invest in real good wins instead of just chase the short term wins. Identify the talkers in the company, keep the ratio and don’t let them push out real good engineers.

reviews.title: Meta Marketing Poo
reviews.pros: Easy once you get into it.  People eventually hardly had to make any calls.  They just would sit around chatting amongst their coworkers and playing chess.  Being in the highest program tier of its kind, I only had 100 clients in my book of business for the quarter.  However, they wanted us to call them 9 times minimum if we otherwise didn't get a "successful" call with them.  There were also spiffs and prizes at times.  I received multiple Amazon gift cards, and other prizes, along with spiffs, one of which over $1000 in a two-week period just for successfully calling 21 people in my shared book.  All you had to do was pitch marketing recommendations, as well as get points from their successful implementation on the advertiser's part.
reviews.cons: We, along with most all other third-party centers around the world, were laid off and the job doesn't exist anymore.
reviews.advice: Be ethical and don't lie.  Do and facilitate the ethical thing for Meta's advertisers.

reviews.title: Toxic and constant layoffs
reviews.pros: Lots of AI integrations - probably ahead of other companies if you want to be more ai native
reviews.cons: You are driven to be productive and efficient at the cost of your own personal growth and employment. Constant layoffs and politics and toxic work environment. With AI expectations are being re calibrated so IC3/4 have a cutoff for promotion but goalpost is moving too. Expect to work a lot 50-60 hours.

reviews.title: Great Pay, Mega Pivots
reviews.pros: They pay really well, and have well defined career growth. The performance review systems are great for motivating people and providing actionable feedback. Can do big things, even if you are on a small team, you can literally reach out to anyone in the company to make / call out problems. The caveat is that your local team's leadership is incentivized to hammer down your concerns and then raise those concerns themselves. Ignore your leadership, and focus on doing the right thing and you will have a grand time.
reviews.cons: This company does not know how to ship a large piece of software and wants immediate results (2-3 months to demonstrate something promising, with an expectation that by another 2-3 months it will ship). If this does not happen they will push your team to develop something different or radically change the thing you are trying to make.
reviews.advice: When developing big software please stop trying to change it to chase a trend. Big software takes a long time and will not be able to ship in time to meet fast trends, big software should be for long term plans.

reviews.title: High bar
reviews.pros: Meta sets an extremely high bar for talent, and being part of People Ops meant working alongside smart, driven, and genuinely capable people. The scale of impact is unmatched, processes, systems, and decisions affect thousands globally, which forces you to think rigorously and operate with discipline. Compensation and benefits are competitive, and there’s real exposure to best-in-class tools, data, and operational frameworks. You learn fast, whether you want to or not.
reviews.cons: The pace can be relentless, and priorities shift quickly based on leadership direction and business needs. People Ops roles, in particular, can feel reactive during periods of change, with limited room to slow down or be fully proactive. Decision-making can be highly top-down, and context isn’t always shared evenly, which can be challenging when you’re expected to execute flawlessly. Burnout is a real risk if boundaries aren’t actively managed.
reviews.advice: Continue investing in transparency and clarity, especially during periods of reorgs or change. People teams are most effective when they’re treated as strategic partners rather than purely executional support. Sustained performance will come from balancing speed with long-term employee trust and well-being.

reviews.title: For the select few, it's great - for everyone else, it's where careers go to die...
reviews.pros: Great benefits, some really interesting people and great career trajectory if you joined 10+ years ago...
reviews.cons:
The culture has become incredibly toxic after all the waves of lay-offs and sudden 'reveals' about performance lay-offs, etc. Feels more like a bad game of Survivor, particularly in the non-Product / Engineering space where it's clear that company leadership wants to eliminate as many roles as quickly as possible to fuel the 'AI cash burning fire'. 

It will also shock you how little autonomy you have compared to other companies you've worked at - I've grown less as a professional here than any other company I've worked at because management just dictates what they want and there's really very little opportunity for growth. 

There's also really no clear strategy other than 'AI everything'. Expect constant pivots (like every 3 months) where you throw out everything you've done to date and start over with new objectives and likely new partners and new management.

VP's are pretty transparently just trying to save their jobs and milk fat RSU payouts as long as possible and talk out of both sides their mouths.
reviews.advice: Have some humility and take the blinders off - the realities on the ground are nothing like what you think they are and are being told most of the time - you've incentivized people to just tell you what you want to hear and that's what they're doing. As a result, there is very little true strategic direction or acknowledgement of business realities, and even more concerningly, often punishment for anyone who tries to be transparent about them. This is particularly the case with AI where data is being cherry picked to 'tell the right' story', with the people doing that knowing they'll have moved on to bigger and better things when the truth finally comes out.

reviews.title: Toxic culture/environment
reviews.pros: Get to play real life squid game yay.
reviews.cons: They keep pushing 15-20% people down to the bottom and manage out(no pip, just direct terminate) every half. Everybody is so anxious that they keep pushing the bar higher, so you are never good enough but keep chasing, Manager has the biggest power, no matter how much work you do, they'll just say impact/visibility is not enough and decide your rating. Some teams have endless SEVs/alerts that feels like oncall 24/7, some teams you don't need to work after 5pm but you can't stop thinking/worry about work(cause finishing planned roadmap is not enough now) so you open the laptop and found everybody else is also working. In 2026, no one is writing a single line of code, we just talk to AI all day, and using AI is part of the performance evaluation. Now a 1 month project is expected to be complete within 1 week due to AI assistance.

reviews.title: Was good, not anymore
reviews.pros:
- strong brand name on resume
- decent pay
reviews.cons:
- constant thrash
- comp hasn't kept pace with other competitors
- bad wlb
- robotic coworkers

reviews.title: Was great to work at, still can be
reviews.pros: The people and the pay
reviews.cons: Culture shifted after the change of government, made the culture more cutthroat

reviews.title: Hectic
reviews.pros:
Money
Perks
Stocks
Resources
Food
reviews.cons: Has the most hectic and stressful day to day work experience

reviews.title: Great Experience overall
reviews.pros: The overall process was phenomenal, the preparation gives you a well rounded idea of what the role actually entails
reviews.cons: I didn't have a negative experience

reviews.title: A lot of politics
reviews.pros: Salary, free food, benefits, everything money related
reviews.cons:
no wlb and managers have too much power
tech is old

reviews.title: Great Job. Unstable Career.
reviews.pros:
- Pay and benefits are top of the market.
- You will feel like you're making a contribution.
- Amazing community and social atmosphere.
- Separation pay is usually 3+ months plus 6+ months of COBRA to soften the blow of sudden termination.
reviews.cons:
- Executive leadership can and will tell you one thing then do another moments later.
- There is no promise of security. There are no criteria to determine whether you will or will not be selected for termination when the company does lay offs. You can be one of the top performing members of your team but for reasons known only to executive leaders, your job can end at a moment's notice.
reviews.advice: Stop changing the entire company ecosystem every 2 - 4 weeks and expecting consistent results. "Embrace Ambiguity" is meant to be advice, not a principal. And please. Tell your staff what the dividing lines are. People would be much more confident in their work knowing they'll be able to finish it before they lose their job.

reviews.title: Great days are in the past
reviews.pros: Compensation and benefits are stellar.
reviews.cons: Diminished value of people contributions.

reviews.title: Ex Startup Program Manager
reviews.pros: Great pay and benefits. Great offices with plenty of amenities. Laid back culture (or at least that's what I felt), where your time was respected and I was not bothered outside of office hours.
reviews.cons:
1. The laid back culture is a 2-way street, where some people took advantage of playing 'busy' and work was not getting done or was pushed over. 
2. Too much talking, not enough execution. I joined Meta thinking I'm joining a pool of highly skilled people and that I'm gonna learn so much. But in my 3 years there 70% of the people I came across were just yappers. Everyone had ideas, no one wanted to execute them.
3. Calls culture - some days were just 8h of calls, out of which 6h could have been short emails. 
4. Objectives can change every 6 months based on how the company's direction changes. Which means you can work 6 months to fulfill your projects and achieve your KPIs and then it can get dropped because it's not a 'goal' anymore.

reviews.title: Great compensation and benefits, but extremely demanding and high-stress environment
reviews.pros:
Compensation is very competitive, especially for new grads
Strong benefits package, including good health insurance
Opportunity to learn quickly due to high ownership and fast pace
The technical challenges are meaningful, and there is a lot to learn across the company.
reviews.cons:
Extremely busy workload; maintaining work-life balance requires constant and intentional effort

Difficult to fully disconnect, as there is always more work to do

High-pressure environment that can lead to sustained stress
Overall atmosphere feels increasingly anxious, which may impact physical and mental well-being over time
Documentation is often limited, so knowledge transfer relies heavily on talking to other people. This can be exhausting, and not everyone has the time or willingness to share a lot of context.

reviews.title: Meta work life balance
reviews.pros: total Comp is high compared to other peer company
reviews.cons:
competitive
Need to survive w/o any help

reviews.title: Bad WLB and great pay and colleague
reviews.pros: Pay is good and colleague is great
reviews.cons: WLB is bad and cannot get any worse

reviews.title: Great pay, high stress
reviews.pros: Great pay, coworkers, interesting tech challenges
reviews.cons: High pressure to perform. Can be team dependent on how well you succeed. A lot of red tape to get projects approved

reviews.title: Zuck is great. But thts where it ends in that layer unfortunately!
reviews.pros: -Great Pay (if you can keep your job)
reviews.cons:
-Work stopped being exciting years ago
-But managers still need to prove their teams are "innovative" and "AI native". so there is lots of pressure on everyone to churn things out. most of which are meaningless AI slop
-Long time people will stick to their long time peers and if you are not part of that click you have no hopes of advancing.
-Join to make money, put a marker in your head and leave with your sanity still intact

reviews.title: wild ride at Meta
reviews.pros: Great flexibility where you can come in when you want. Also, met really nice co-workers.
reviews.cons:
I was a sourcer on pipelining candidates for  gen SWE (frontend, full stack, backend, mobile and ML/AI engineers).  There were several hundred of us doing looking for the same candidate profiles.  It was not hard to find candidates but finding ones not claimed was the challenge. It felt like one gigantic agency.  Some people were nice and others not so much. One of the colleagues joked that  it's the "hunger games" in recruiting.

There's constant changes from switching hiring pillars to weekly changes on FBR (ATS), often times no clear reason as to why there's changes.  

The goals were so tough that it's not reachable for the majority of the folks there.  There's no way one can work 40 hour week and reach the hiring goals.

It was tough seeing colleagues being cut on a weekly basis---this created a toxic culture.

reviews.title: Helpful review
reviews.pros: Rotational, can try out multiple teams
reviews.cons: Poor management, unclear feedback, all depends on if company wants to cut costs

reviews.title: Overall good
reviews.pros: smart people, free food, and lots of learning
reviews.cons: competitive and always wanting to do your best or else cut off, high stress but good benefits to make up for it,

reviews.title: Fast culture
reviews.pros:
1. You work like in startup culture
2. Complete freedom
reviews.cons: 1. Have to be very fast to be up to date

reviews.title: Could be interesting work, horrible culture
reviews.pros: Lots of problems to solve, very large scale. Okay pay but now on par with most companies maybe a 10%/15% bump not like it was before 2021.
reviews.cons: Highly over processed, you spend all day updating trackers and providing status updates and taking useless trainings or creating them for optics. The performance exits and layoffs have ruined the culture. It has created a low trust enviroment. People actively try to undermine you and/or are jealous of your success. Managers are useless. They don't do any coaching. They give no direction and expect you to get things right, the first time.
reviews.advice: Get your head out of your buts. Talk to you employees and actually listen. Stop firing the worker bees and instead look at the "managers" first. Then the team leads and so on. Fire toxic people first.

reviews.title: It's a meat grinder
reviews.pros: Great for resume as it gives you lots of attention with recruiters
reviews.cons: Bad for morale. Constant threat of layoffs

reviews.title: Awesome work environment, careful for your growth
reviews.pros: Very good at overall work pay, talents you can meet. Opportunities you will have.
reviews.cons: Be careful about growth because you don't know if you are in politics or not when you head to your goal.
